title: Fault-tolerant measurement-device-independent quantum key distribution with noisy non-Gaussian error correction
abstract: It is well known that the repeater node is an essential ingredient for the future global quantum network, which will enable high-rate private communication and entanglement distribution over very long distances. The near-term repeater architecture uses the measurement-based node that operate without both entanglement and quantum memory, which is the main idea of the measurement-device-independent quantum key distribution (MDI-QKD) protocol. The MDI-QKD protocol removes the trust condition from the inter repeaters, while its continuous variable (CV) version, when proposed, benefited from its deterministic nature, compatible with the classical devices, and shows a high rate for the short-range local area network (LAN). Whilst the theoretical backbone of CV-MDI-QKD protocol is well established, its secure transmission range is yet limited for practical LAN. In this study, we propose an enhanced scheme for the asymmetric CV-MDI-QKD protocol by using Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) oscillators-to-oscillators codes, where both loss error and operation error are suppressed to below the break-even point, without any delays caused by classical heralding signals. In particular, the proposed scheme, which correlates the noises of the data and ancilla via a pair of symplectic transforms, extracts the error syndromes from stabilizer measurements on the ancilla mode and informs the data mode for a corrective displacement. Numerical analysis shows the composable finite-size security of the protocol under the collective Gaussian attack, encompassing noiseless and noisy GKP states, with both wired (i.e., fiber-based) and wireless (i.e., free-space) configurations. In addition, we demonstrate that the residual errors of the GKP code can be further reduced by the concatenation method but has a trade-off between the layers number and the finite GKP squeezing.
summary: The study proposes a fault-tolerant CV-MDI-QKD protocol using GKP error correction to overcome the range limitations of current continuous-variable networks. By using non-Gaussian codes and a concatenation strategy, the researchers show that both loss and noise can be suppressed, significantly extending the secure communication distance in both fiber and wireless settings.
main_problem: The limited secure transmission range of continuous-variable measurement-device-independent quantum key distribution (CV-MDI-QKD) due to sensitivity to loss and additive Gaussian noise.
main_result: The proposed GKP-based error correction scheme suppresses both loss and operation errors below the break-even point, extending the maximum secure transmission distance in both fiber and free-space links.
method: An enhanced asymmetric CV-MDI-QKD protocol using Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) oscillator-to-oscillator codes and a one-by-one concatenation method to extract error syndromes via ancilla measurements.
model_or_system: An asymmetric CV-MDI-QKD network topology featuring an untrusted relay connecting Alice and Bob, utilizing coherent states, GKP ancilla modes, and Two-Mode Squeezed (TMS) operations across fiber and wireless (fading) channels.
key_observables: Secret key rate (asymptotic and composable finite-size), residual error variance, mutual information, and Holevo information.
important_parameters: GKP squeezing levels (e.g., 20 dB), channel transmittance, fiber attenuation (0.2 dB/km), and number of concatenation layers.
main_assumptions: Security is analyzed against collective Gaussian attacks using the quantum de Finetti theorem; the use of a linear estimation function for error syndromes instead of MMSE.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the network topology and protocol schematic; Figure 2 compares key rates against the PLOB bound; Figure 3 illustrates the GKP error correction architecture and residual error variance; Figure 5 shows RCI and finite-size key rates for ideal and noisy GKP states; Figure 6 shows the PDF of error variance in fading channels.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CV-MDI-QKD limitation, proposes a GKP-based error correction scheme using TMS operations, provides a detailed security analysis under collective attacks, evaluates performance in fiber and free-space (fading) channels, and discusses the optimization of concatenated layers.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates how non-Gaussian error correction (GKP codes) can be used to combat Gaussian noise and loss in bosonic channels, a central theme in developing robust quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Network topology using asymmetric CV MDI-QKD protocol with an untrusted relay R (using active optical switch). LAN: local area network. (b) The PM scheme of the CV-MDI-QKD protocol. Time flows from left to right, while the single lines and double lines represent the bosonic modes and classical variables, respectively. |0⟩denotes the vacuum state. The block D is the displacement operator. The block U is Gaussian unitary involving two links. The relay R performs some (in principle unknown) physical transformation to output γ and gives to the eavesdropper Eve quantum side information. The raw keys of Alice and Bob are (qx, px) and (qy, py), respectively.

FIG. 2. Asymptotic key rate versus fiber distance of Bob-to- relay with reverse reconciliation. The solid lines and dashed lines represent the original configuration and the configura- tion with the pre-amplification operator APre 1/τA, respectively. The dash-dotted line denotes the PLOB bound when LA = 0 km.

FIG. 3. (a) Schematic of the protocol with GKP error correction on the Alice side. The blue lines and orange lines represent the data mode and ancilla mode, respectively. The GKP codes encode the data mode by a Gaussian unitary Senc. The overall AWGN channel Φ¯nB+1−τA consists of amplifier APre 1/τA and thermal-loss channel LτA,¯nB. The error syndromes s are extracted from stabilizer measurements on the ancilla mode and inform the data mode for a corrective displacement Df(s). (b) The Wigner function of the ideal square GKP state. (c) Architecture of the TMS operation. B1/2 denotes the two-mode beamsplitter with transmittance 1/2. S (r) denotes the one-mode squeezer with the squeezing...

FIG. 4. The equivalent entanglement-based scheme with error correction. The block U ′ is unitary involving links, am- plifier and error correction. The symbol ψTMSV denotes the TMSV state. By heterodyning mode a (b), Alice remotely prepares a coherent state |α⟩(|β⟩) on mode A (B). The out- comes ˜α and ˜β have a linear relationship with the projected amplitude α and β, respectively.

FIG. 5. (a) RCI, or equivalently, the lower bound on the optimal key rate versus both Alice-to-relay and Bob-to-relay distance when using GKP code. (b) Composable finite-size key rate versus both Alice-to-relay and Bob-to-relay distance when using GKP code. The green surface denotes the secure region of the protocol with the QT compaction method (20 dB TMSV squeezing). QT: quantum teleportation. (c) Composable finite-size key rate versus block size. The grey line denotes the typical value of the block size (i.e., N = 108) used in this work. (d) Composable finite-size key rate versus Bob-to-relay distance with one-by-one concatenation method.
